sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Recurrent chest pain.
History of Present Illness
Evelyn is a 56-year-old female with a past medical history of coronary artery disease, prior carpal tunnel release, and a herniated disc. She presents for her annual exam. Over the last month, she has been experiencing a bit of chest pain, which she describes as a burning sensation, and it occurs when she walks. The chest pain is not severe and goes away if she rests. She reports nasal congestion due to allergies. She denies any lower extremity edema or muscle aches or fever. She has had no recurrence of her carpal tunnel symptoms and reports full range of motion. She has been doing well with her back pain, with no back pain or limitations in movement.
Findings
Physical exam reveals a slight 3/6 systolic ejection murmur. Lung sounds are clear. No carotid bruits are appreciated. No lower extremity edema. The echocardiogram from last year showed a slightly low pumping function of 45%. There was also mitral regurgitation. The ECG is stable and reflects her prior coronary artery disease. Her blood pressure is well controlled.
Assessment
The patient has known coronary artery disease and is experiencing recurrent chest pain. She has a history of mitral regurgitation and a slightly reduced ejection fraction.
Plan

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value: The doctor ordered a stress test to evaluate the chest pain. The patient will continue on aspirin and toprol. A lipid panel will be ordered and Crestor 20 mg will be started. A follow-up appointment will be scheduled in four weeks if symptoms persist.
